posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
I wonder how big a role in wine criticism, the geographical origin of the critic plays a role, in shaping their perspective and influencing their preferences. William Kelley/The wine advocate has recently released his champagne reviews, which invite scrutiny regarding the impact of one’s regional background on wine critique. In a wine world sadly dominated by anglocentric influences, the question of whether a critic can free themselves from their regional bias is pertinent.

Observations on William Kelley’s champagne reviews reveal a persistent inclination towards Burgundian stylistic influences. While his criticism remains within acceptable boundaries and the point inflation is in check, it becomes increasingly evident that his affinity for Burgundian winemaking principles prejudices his assessment of champagnes. This bias, while perhaps unintentional, results in the omission of reviews for many champagne producers across various quality tiers, consequently distorting the overall perspective.

Vinous has also recently presented comprehensive champagne reviews, raising questions about the impact of regional background. Monika Kriebehl’s (MW) at Vinous initially sparked skepticism in me as although she comes from Germany, I feared her anglo training would not be beneficial considering the on going debate surrounding the anglocentric elitism within the MW community, which can alienate certain perspectives.

However, Vinous’ approach to champagne reviews has pleasantly surpised me. Antonio Galloni, known for his passionate/emotional style and sometimes hyperbolic style, offers in my mind a more balanced and open-minded assessment of wines and champagnes. His emotional tasting approach seems to transcend linguistic and regional boundaries, resulting in a distinctive perspective.

Anne Kriebehl’s approach also defies expectations. Rather than adhering dogmatically to a particular style, she displays an unexpected open-mindedness. Her willingness to explore diverse perspectives stands in contrast to the stereotype of certian MWs, rigid in their views.

In conclusion, William Kelley’s champagne reviews may resonate with those whose palates align with his Burgundian preferences, basically he is preaching to the converted, conservative wine drinker, one will not discover anything new in his reviews.

However, I think for those seeking a more impartial and diverse assessment, Antonio Galloni and Vinous offer in my mind a refreshing departure from anglocentric influences. The days of viewing champagne solely through Burgundian lenses may limit the potential for a truly informed opinion, highlighting the importance of a critic’s ability to transcend regional biases in the pursuit of wine excellence.

Thanks Donald. As you have said many times, the world of Champagne is vast and the number of producers reviewed by the professionals is small. This limits the diversity of points of view, for sure, but I too have valued the recently released reviews in these English language online sources.

As someone who only reads these sources (and only a few of those) I’m curious what the writers in other markets are saying that’s so different.

By the way, the Vinous reviewer’s first name is Anne. And I too have found her reviews to be interesting, clear and open-minded.

posts.cooked:
I have being doing a lot of research for a blog article to help consumers understand critics and how they align to personal preferences. One would really have to throw in the German champange critic into the mix Gerhard Eichelmann, but the german language is obvioulsy a barrier. Also I think Tyson Stelzer is a superior champagne critic to both Galloni and Kelley especially when it comes to the maisons, but one wonders about his partiality as he hosts it seems a lot of dinners.

It just really struck me looking through the Wine advocate reviews, how it was basically one style of champagnes that were being reviewed.

I think it is a bit like Vinous, Josh Reynolds was a really good reviewer of champagnes as was David Schildknecht but it seems this is a job reserved for the chef. I think The wine advocate has better reviewers of champagne, namely Stephan Reihardt.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
Weil ich Champagner verkaufe, bin ich jede Woche in der Champagne-Region, und ich denke einfach nicht, dass er ein ausgewogener Kritiker ist. Für mich ist es ein bisschen so, als würde man sagen, du hast einen Kritiker von Shakespeares Werken, und er hat nur, sagen wir, 10 Stücke gelesen, qualifiziert ihn das, ein Kritiker von Shakespeare zu sein?

Was die Kritiker mit Bordeaux oder Burgund machen, kümmert mich nicht wirklich, aber bei Champagner ist das anders.

Das erste Stück handelt nicht von William Kelley, sondern vom Unterschied zwischen Vinous und dem Wine Advocate, und es lässt mich darüber nachdenken, wenn man vergleicht, wer der bessere Kritiker ist, und für mich würde ich sagen, Antonio Galloni. Ich meine, wir in Deutschland haben das Glück, Gerhard Eichelmann zu haben, einen Kritiker mit tiefem Wissen und Erfahrung, nicht jemanden, der fragwürdige Flaschen bei eBay kauft.

Because I sell champagne, I am in the Champagne region every week and I just do not think he is a balanced critic. For me it is a bit like say you have a critic of Shakespeares works and he has only read say 10 plays, does that qualify him to be a critic of Shakespeare.

What the critics do with Bordeaux or Burgundy does not really bother me, but with Champagne it is different.

The first piece is not about William Kelley but the difference between Vinous and the Wine advocate, and it makes me wonder when one compares, who is the better critic and for me I would say Antonio Galloni.I mean we in Germany are lucky to have Gerhard Eichelmann, a critic with profound knowledge and experience, not someone buying dubious bottles on ebay.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
I recently discovered a thing called Topical map, this is for me really interesting and it is really good for the website. One of the suggestions is to discuss critics, explain where they are coming from. I think it is really interesting to see the different perspectives and from where critics are coming from.

My favourite critic is Gerhard Eichelmann, it is a shame the english book was not such a success but the new edition is being translated into french at the request of the producers.

If people think I am critical of William Kelley then they do not know how much Neal Martin loves me. I think it is interesting Vinous and The wine advocate released at roughlythe same time. I was not expecting much from Vinous and I have visited master classes which Kriebehl held on Alsatian riesling which were very strange. But this time Vinous really delivered, the points are down and the texts are good. I feared prodcuers leaning more towards the natural genre would be treated like Gibbs MW treated those in the Loire, but this was not the case.

William Kelley can correct me if I am wrong, but I recall him saying he struggled with champagnes stlyes which were not to his preference. This is OK, and if a customer came to me and said he liked the burugndian aspect of champagne, then I would point him in the way of Willaim Kelley.

One of the good things about champagne is the diversity, the burgundian aspect is only one side and sadly there are too many young producers trying to go down this path without really knowing what they were doing. For ma good example of this is Adrien Renoir, I felt with his 18 releases he was starting to find his style and I was really looking forward to the 19 releases. We tasted the 2019 terroir a few weeks ago and i was really disappointed. I decided not to post on it but the interesting thing here is the stylisitc. Obvioulsy this is a style which please William kelley as he gave it 93 points, personally I would go for 90 poits but that is spliiting hairs.

What i find espcially with Domaine du Tuilleries, is they are also going for that style but they pull it off. Obvioulsy this is a individual theory but Renoir just does not have the concentration or is not doing enough batonnage which I would say Tuilleries are. In terms of price and as some one who sells last year I wold hve recommended Renoir, this year i can muster no enthusiasm.

PS Otto, I have prompted Chatgpt to add 15% spelling mistake and put in more language varianz

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I think my palate is quite a bit more diverse than that, Donald. If you read the reviews you will see favorable appreciations for styles as different as Fabrice Gas and J-M Séléque, Selosse and Pierre Péters, ranging from tank fermentation with a reductive élevage to barrel fermentation with an oxidative/biological élevage.

Of course I do have preferences, and as @Andy_Sc observes, I consider the notion of stylistic agnosticism in wine criticism to be a dead end. After all, rating wines is inherently about preferring some to others, and how to do so if you disavow having taste? My tastes make my work more coherent.

In Champagne, I make no secret of preferring wines made from concentrated, physiologically mature grapes, which deliver wines with textural dimension, intense flavors, the capacity to evolve with genuine interest (as opposed to Maillard-derived smoke and mirrors), integrated acidity, and the need for only minimal dosage. Within those parameters, huge stylistic diversity is possible and I tend to embrace it. This set of preferences is also in harmony with what one finds in the benchmark Champagnes of the 50s, 60s and on into the 70s, before the full effects of over-productive clones and intensive farming techniques deprived many Champagnes of their texture and concentration.

On the other hand, I do not like Champagnes that are variously dilute; shrill; excessively high in dosage to compensate for poor quality fruit; or marked by organoleptic defects such as mousse taint or turbidity. I do not think this set of dislikes is that rare among wine lovers, which is presumably why some of them are kind enough to follow my work.

And as for the article being conservative, again, I simply don’t agree. If you can find other English-language critics covering the likes of Lurquin, Lamblot, Aurélien Clément, the Ponsart brother, Maxime Oudiette and Gaspard Brochet, I would love to know about it. And these reviews are not based on importer portfolio tastings but on going to the cellars and seeing the vineyards.

Galloni is an odd poster boy for integrity: he sells ’bespoke’ advance prescriptions to those in the trade, including shops like yours. Donald, do you have an early access subscription?

I’m not slagging Galloni only for his ethically-challenged subscription model. When he took over much of California from Parker, Galloni cynically heaped praise on the most unpalatable, disgusting Cali Cabs and Rhone knock-offs as had his then boss. There was no way the guy who only praised traditionalists in Italy would voluntarily ingest many of the California wines he sold to rubes (TWA subscribers) under the guise of rating them.

As do others, I find your Champagne posts to be interesting. I also find your ad-hominem attacks of Kelley to reek of envy. Don’t be a knob. Thank you.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked:
As I mentioned earlier in this topic, I thought Donald’s latest comments were veering into a far more reasonable place. Discussing and comparing critics, highlighting perceived flaws or weaknesses of them, and anointing favorites is tried and true subject matter for WB. I’m interested in this as I read reviewers and try to glean information from them. In the postings here Donald is trying really hard to discuss differences and (his) preferences, and leave off the random ad hominem attacks.

So I applaud the attempt, even if he isn’t right. Like many here, I read a lot of Donald’s posts with interest, and wonder at his commitment/need to work out his personal issues here as well as talk about wine. And I cheer even more to see William join in the discussion, explain his POV, his tastes and his approach. Argument becoming discussion is not the normal path for Internet boards. William expands his role to critic/interlocutor/psychoanalyst.

Funny side note. I recently got an offer from a wine retailer with quotes from Donald’s posts about the wines. Comical for a retailer to use another retailer’s notes to sell wine. Next thing you know he will give up wine sales for criticism.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
People here get me wrong. I find the discussions here more stimulating than those on Vinous, but Antonio Galloni really surprised me with his champagne review. Sure, one can criticize Galloni for selling his reviews in advance, but on The Wine Advocate, they list shops and other locations where one can buy the wines, so I am not sure how that plays into things.

The problem is inherent in this post; one tries to have a reasoned debate and suddenly it is all about William Kelley. If William Kelley were Malfoy, then you have the importer Greg K jumping in as Goyle. I really like this phrase (as opposed to Maillard-derived smoke and mirrors), and this would be worthy of a discussion in itself.

Then there is the thing about my anglophobia. Since the catastrophe of 2016 and then the bigger catastrophe of the 31st of January 2020, I just cannot be objective about anything English, especially individuals deriving from those privileged backgrounds and ruining everything. It is with an intense feeling of schadenfreude that I watch that privileged class annihilate themselves and, sadly, bring down ‘plebs’ like myself in their self-destruction.

A critic I also really like is the Wine King, and I am warming to Konstantin Baum despite his MW title. I just have to figure out how to integrate William Kelley authentically into the shops to benefit from the keywords, that is something for Chatgpt to do next year. But basically, Eichelmann, with his catholic taste as a critic, puts more bums on seats just as Bill Nanson is the best critic for selling Burgundy. Peter Leim for me is more of an educator. I really like Tyson Stelzer, the only one with the guts to call out Selosse, something neither Galloni or William Kelley has done.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
I think all of us on the board post opinions that contain bias and do not always fly true to the mark.

Part of the board is about either choosing to disregard posts we disagree with, or to refine or dispute the merit of the post by defining the bias for others to see.

Suggesting that posters should be quiet and not post unless they have 100% comprehension of the subject would leave not a single person on this board in a position to post anything, including you.

Does the OP have a bias with William Kelley’s champagne reviews? It would certainly seem so.

Is everything he says completely baseless nonsense? That, given the real world challenges of critical review and the OP’s reasonably knowledgeable posts on champagne, seems unlikely too.

Does that mean I should disregard WK’s reviews on Champagne? Not at all.

I like William Kelley and enjoy his posting here quite a bit. The board is MUCH better off for having him posting here. But I feel that having a contrary view doesn’t affect that in the slightest, rather it points out that he’s human like the rest of us, and whether I agree or disagree with the OP I can consider whether or not parts of what he’s posting are things to think about. And make my own decision like an adult.

As much as I disagree with much of Donal’s haranguing of WK here on the board, I disagree considerably more with shushing dissenting voices and building sacred cows.

I have yet to see a critic (or winemaker or person) that is universally free from bias, and it should certainly stand out to any thinking person that William Kelley’s popularity on this board is likely in part due to his biases simply mirroring our own more than most other critics. Which for me is great, and why I enjoy his thoughts so much (being US-anglo and all).

That said, the MW certification is archaic. With the expansion of wine production globally, it’s physically impossible for a single person to have mastery of all regions. No more than a single person could be a “master” of medicine or law. There’s simply too many wines and too many places, and more diversification by a factor of 1000 than when the program was begun. At some point, regional review makes more sense (Jancis Robinson has Samantha Cole-Johnson writing on the Willamette Valley for her) but regions that have reviewers of note living in them (and in WKs situation making wine in them) do enjoy a significant bias/luxury. That’s life…it’s not fair. But as long as people/critics do their best to determine the quality of the wines the review, it will work. But knowing that these issues exist should be important for any thinking person.

My reasoning why your opinion is wrong: As 9 out of 10 wine drinkers have clear style preferences, the reviews of a critic without style preference have little value for most wine drinkers as the consumer needs to decipher the tasting notes to find out if the wine fits their style preference or not and often the notes are not clear enough to convey that (especially as everybody uses different descriptors). So in my book, the best critics out there are the critics with a bias, a style preference.

posts.name: J0hn J K@ne
posts.cooked:
D_Pennet:

I wonder how big a role in wine criticism, the geographical origin of the critic plays a role, in shaping their perspective and influencing their preferences. William Kelley/The wine advocate has recently released his champagne reviews, which invite scrutiny regarding the impact of one’s regional background on wine critique. In a wine world sadly dominated by anglocentric influences, the question of whether a critic can free themselves from their regional bias is pertinent.

Observations on William Kelley’s champagne reviews reveal a persistent inclination towards Burgundian stylistic influences. While his criticism remains within acceptable boundaries and the point inflation is in check, it becomes increasingly evident that his affinity for Burgundian winemaking principles prejudices his assessment of champagnes. This bias, while perhaps unintentional, results in the omission of reviews for many champagne producers across various quality tiers, consequently distorting the overall perspective.

Vinous has also recently presented comprehensive champagne reviews, raising questions about the impact of regional background. Monika Kriebehl’s (MW) at Vinous initially sparked skepticism in me as although she comes from Germany, I feared her anglo training would not be beneficial considering the on going debate surrounding the anglocentric elitism within the MW community, which can alienate certain perspectives.

However, Vinous’ approach to champagne reviews has pleasantly surpised me. Antonio Galloni, known for his passionate/emotional style and sometimes hyperbolic style, offers in my mind a more balanced and open-minded assessment of wines and champagnes. His emotional tasting approach seems to transcend linguistic and regional boundaries, resulting in a distinctive perspective.

Anne Kriebehl’s approach also defies expectations. Rather than adhering dogmatically to a particular style, she displays an unexpected open-mindedness. Her willingness to explore diverse perspectives stands in contrast to the stereotype of certian MWs, rigid in their views.

In conclusion, William Kelley’s champagne reviews may resonate with those whose palates align with his Burgundian preferences, basically he is preaching to the converted, conservative wine drinker, one will not discover anything new in his reviews.

However, I think for those seeking a more impartial and diverse assessment, Antonio Galloni and Vinous offer in my mind a refreshing departure from anglocentric influences. The days of viewing champagne solely through Burgundian lenses may limit the potential for a truly informed opinion, highlighting the importance of a critic’s ability to transcend regional biases in the pursuit of wine excellence.

Donald,

First, I want to start by saying that I have enjoyed some of the wines I’ve tried on your recommendation, including Domaine de Tuiliers, though I disagreed somewhat with the spectacularly high rating you provided the 2019 Acacia. I appreciate many of your contributions in the Champagne thread.

Next, I’d like to say that I have not appreciated a number of what seem like self-interested takedowns, or kind of personal attacks on palates when folks disagree with your position. We’ve lost a long-time contributor to Wineberserkers who was very active on the Champagne thread because of some unnecessary personal comments from you. Do I think the individual should have had more fortitude and stuck around? Yes. Can your poorly veiled personal attacks wear on folks? Also yes.

The William Kelley vendetta, along with the gross generalizations and demeaning comments, makes you come across at times like a sore and petulant child. Spiteful and maybe a bit jealous. Because of its frequency and consistency, this post can’t really be read as a desire for an open discussion. It’s just an opinion piece: These are my favorite critics and here’s why, and I think William Kelley is a twat.

We get it. Spare us. I read plenty of Vinous and Advocate champagne reviews. Even Wine Spectator, Decanter, and sometimes, god forbid, James Suckling. Sometimes I agree with one critic and not another. Sometimes on a separate wine I agree with the other critic, and not another. Some of the critics you’ve mentioned that you like, I think write poor notes, often with literally no flavor descriptors, or with descriptors so out there I cannot reasonably believe we’re tasting the same thing, even if we end up at the same overall assessment of quality.

But no one thinks you’re on here trying to find the right critic for you. And at this point no one is foolish enough to believe you’re trying to help them, sans bias, find the right critic for their palate.

Chill, pop a bottle, let us know if it’s one you carry or not, then give your honest assessment. I assure you that’s more valuable than whatever this was intended to be.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
It is wrong to consistently bash Willaim Kelley, and one has to applaud him for participating in discussion.

Over the past two months, a growing sense of frustration has permeated our customer base. Customers who were once able to afford a wide range of wines now find themselves priced out of the market, and their complaints have become more pronounced. Efforts to encourage them to explore alternative options have yielded limited success.

One noteworthy incident involved a customer who, after a 15-minute discussion with me, arrived at a significant realization: perhaps I am not the ideal person to address their concerns. Instead, he proposed that it should be the producers, as well as wine critics, who should hear these grievances. He even expressed a willingness to have his concerns recorded and shared with both wine critics and producers. This pivotal moment sparked an innovative idea.

With the guidance of someone well-versed in how such hotlines operate, we have crafted a concept. It entails empowering consumers to directly communicate their complaints or compliments not only to the producers but also to wine critics, bypassing the intermediary. Furthermore, it allows producers and wine critics to provide explanations for the frequent price increases.

In light of this innovative approach, I would like to reach out to William Kelley and inquire whether such an idea would be of interest. Or could I record these conversations and pass them on, it would make my life considerably easier and less frustrating.

posts.name: Cris Whetstone

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
There is a real question about wine pricing today, but for me it is a lot more complex than “wines have become unaffordable”. In reality, even a 20 EUR bottle is unaffordable to most people in the world, so what we are really saying is that “wines have increased in price relative to how they used to be priced to an extent that is now uncomfortable for the reasonably affluent Western European middle classes”. Put like this, it is harder to feel quite so much moral outrage.

To me, while there are some wines today that are far too expensive, there are plenty that are too cheap. The legacy of the agrochemical revolution of the second half of the 20th century was a lot of cheap food and also wine; but this came at the expense of quality. The question is, do you want excellence or are you happy with a compromise? In many regions, consumers prefer the latter, but for whatever reason, they are willing to pay for excellence in Champagne and Burgundy. Let’s pencil out the costs of making a wine more or less without compromise, just in terms of labor and materials, for a vineyard planted at 10,000 vines per hectare in the Côte d’Or:

5250 bottles per hectare @ 40 hl/ha
unit cost EUR per hectare per bottle
manual work (hours) 1000 16 16000 3.05
harvesting (hours) 175 11.52 2016 0.38
barrels (used) 17.5 400 7000 1.33
corks (best qualiy) 5250 1.5 7875 1.50
glass per bottle 5250 1 5250 1.00
treatments 12 75 900 0.17
tractor work (hours) 200 50 10000 1.90
labels 5250 0.5 2625 0.50
capsules 5250 0.3 1575 0.30
10.14
plus taxes 12.68

Here I’ve assumed that you can pay your harvesters the minimum wage; that you use organic treatments but just copper and sulfur, none of the expensive essential oils etc that can partially replace copper; and no new oak.

I also haven’t accounted for the costs of any materiel, premises, energy costs, full time salaried employees, the cost of servicing any loans, the cost of tying up capital in wine in élevage (for 18 months if you want two winters on the lees). I certainly haven’t taken into account the land values (3 million per hectare for Meursault village, for example) or the cost of sharecropping agreements. And nor do I factor in the opportunity cost of not selling the fruit on the bulk market, which is riding very high at the moment.

If you add on a profit margin for the producer at let’s say the same margin for which you run your business, and then the 100+% markup by the importer/retailer, it is very difficult to put the bottle on the shelf or the table for less than 50 EUR. And you’ll understand that producers in the Côte d’Or who actually put the same effort into their regional and village level wines as they do their higher appellations are actually subsidizing those lower appellations with their top wines. What’s more, of that 50 EUR, the largest single share will be the margin of the importer/retailer.

Which of the lines above would you prefer to compromise on? It’s easy to reduce any of those costs. But those also reduce the quality of the wine, and the quality of the environment we inhabit in many cases. Or the quality of life of the producers. Should wine growers really be deprived of a level of quality of life similar to that of their clients?

We devote a lot of time to criticizing producers, but no one stops to ask if it’s really reasonable to put coefficients of 100+% on 80+ EUR bottles? Which isn’t to devalorize the role of distribution at all, which has very big costs too, and plays a very important part. But as a merchant, if you object to a producer asking for 10 more EUR per bottle, how do you justify then asking the consumer for 15 EUR more on top of that? Are you having to do any more work? Why don’t we ask these questions when we talk about wine prices?

unit cost EUR per hectare per bottle
manual work (hours) 2500 16 40000 13.79
harvesting (hours) 175 11.52 2016 0.70
barrels (used) 9.5 850 8075 2.78
corks (best qualiy) 2900 2 5800 2.00
glass per bottle 2900 1 2900 1.00
treatments 12 75 900 0.31
horse culitvation 200 100 20000 6.90
labels 2900 0.5 1450 0.50
capsules 2900 0.3 870 0.30
28.28
plus taxes 35.35

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
The second scenario also factors in animal traction for working the soil, and you could replace the tractor for treatments with a chenillard for about the same. And the yield went down, so price per bottle of all the more or less fixed costs (picking for example, and manual work per vine) goes up. I also took the cork cost up to 2 EUR a pop to have the best of the best of the best.

So you see why it is tempting to make a big crop…

And why people working with Grenache vines at 3,300 vines per hectare in a climate where they only need to do three treatments a year and don’t need to regularly cultivate the soils (and can do so with a normal agricultural tractor so the don’t need such expensive labor) do not have comparable production costs!

posts.name: Peter Chiu

posts.name: Knut Sogner
posts.cooked: This is the old labour value-argument put forward by Adam Smith and Karl Marx that has been dead for more than 150 years. The rise in fine wine prices has absolutely nothing to do the labour costs, it is based on demand, and quite probably an unhealthy dose of speculative demand. Buying these wines in large quantities like before for us traditional consimers is not just a question of not affording to, but about relative value. Increasingly we are looking elsewhere, and will continue to do so. In the meantime Burgundy and other «high rise in prices» produsers will experience the gradual erosion of their traditional drinkers-customers, and can only hope that their new buyers are there to stay in a world that has evolved from 0 % interest rate to a biting 5.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
A few years ago for some reason I received an email from the german VDP that was imploring producers to make wines at a price class between 7€ and 15 €. When I see how much wine is being discounted in Germany, into these price classes I would imagine that is where demand is.

As some with a background in michelin gastronomy, to start off one thinks three star restaurant that is the target. But then one sees the costs, the sacrifice and at the end of the day does it pay off. Doubtful. So if I have Bib Gourmand should I stive for a michelin star, more costs more labour intensive and today no guarantuee of a real increase in revenue.

I think in fine wine it is similair to fine art, how many great wines can really exist just as how many great artists can exist. Does a market really exist, does putting in the investment really pay off. How many chefs go through the michelin system and really make it to the top, how many artists go through the art schools and make it to the top. And I think it is the same with champagne and wine.

I think it comes back to michelin example, sure you can put the work in which might make the wine 15€ more expensive but is there a market for it. The market always decides and I don’t think the market is there.

I am on the frontline and am a grumpy bugger because I have to deal with grumpy buggers.

With Parker a really good wine under 20€ with 90 points meant something, today it is meaningless.

William, with the greatest respect, you cater for the luxury market and are losing site of the basis. It is probably a real twist of fate that Parker started out as an advocate for the wine drinkers and freed wine criticism from the elite snobbish english wine writers and now it is a luxury rag, La révolution dévore ses enfants.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked: I have long given up on trying to be an aspirational epicurean, I have no chance of achieving ataraxia.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
Donald, the reason fine wine prices exploded in the past 30 years is because a limited supply (there is not more fine wine produced in Bdx, Champagne or Burgundy) is met by an ever growing affluent middle class (China alone added 300-400 millions to the middle class in the last 30 years) and ever more rich people (the number of millionaires out there is 30-40 times higher than 30 years ago). Simple supply and demand. To believe that a critic score is the reason for the price increases shows a lack of understanding of basic economics.

As a retailer, you just have to find the clients that have the money and are willing to pay the price. The client of tomorrow is often not the client of yesterday. That’s the name of the game. It always has been. And if prices climb too fast and too far, the market will say stop and prices will come down again.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked: I think it is just to simple to reduce every thing down to supply and demand. I think information asymmetry plays a large part in this.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: It’s nothing of the kind, it’s just about what you can’t get if you don’t pay for it. What the market does afterwards is an entirely different matter.

posts.name: Al Osterheld
posts.cooked:

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
D_Pennet:

I think it is just to simple to reduce every thing down to supply and demand. I think information asymmetry plays a large part in this.

100% agree. Information assymetry always played a big role. For many years, there was a huge information assymetry: Insiders who visited the region and had good relationships with knowledgable retailers were able to purchase the best Burgundies or Champagnes for so little money. These days, with so many critics and in addition so much information on social media, as well as wine-searcher showing all wine drinkers where to buy every wine, this information asymmetry has gone down (or vanished). No longer the insiders buy the wine, but those with deep pockets buy the wine. It’s a sad development, but it’s a what happened. The information assymetry is gone…

posts.name: Knut Sogner
posts.cooked: Of course it is. That producers today (or, probably, yesterday) can sit and add cost piece by piece without consequence is just a result of how market forces have made that possible. They get an illusion of their price-defining authority. I will, of course, since I am a long-term follower of how quality of wine has progressed, agree that rise in wine quality impact demand. But there are limits - or a world of demand choices - that is starting to appear to will affect som regions. Many things will affect the wine prices in the future, but labour costs will not be one of them.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
D_Pennet:

Over the past two months, a growing sense of frustration has permeated our customer base. Customers who were once able to afford a wide range of wines now find themselves priced out of the market, and their complaints have become more pronounced. Efforts to encourage them to explore alternative options have yielded limited success.

One noteworthy incident involved a customer who, after a 15-minute discussion with me, arrived at a significant realization: perhaps I am not the ideal person to address their concerns. Instead, he proposed that it should be the producers, as well as wine critics, who should hear these grievances

I find this both funny and amazing because selling wine to consumers is quite literally the only job that wine retailers have!

If the customer thinks that his concerns are best addressed by other people, the wine retailer may have delegated upwards, but has fundamentally failed at his job. If the customer is so reliant on the scores of a critic, that means the retailer has been using those scores to sell wines in the first place - live by the critical acclaim, die by the critical acclaim.

A good craftsman does not blame his tools.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
Break down the text into a primitve and vulgar form and then you have the original prompt.

You know Greg, I love Chatgpt, it helped us really boost the business and at the same helped us save so much money which ultimately benifits the consumer.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
This is irony increased by 10%: It is indeed a remarkable feat that an individual has managed to compose such an extensive volume of text, which, upon closer examination, appears to contribute remarkably little in terms of substantive value.

And then by 50%. It is both astonishing and somewhat perplexing how an individual has succeeded in producing such an expansive corpus of text, which, under rigorous scrutiny, reveals an unexpectedly modest contribution to meaningful discourse, defying expectations of substantive depth.

Just ask chat gpt to go into lawyer modus and that is what it spews.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Knut_Sogner:

Many things will affect the wine prices in the future, but labour costs will not be one of them.

Knut, would you mind explaining why the labor involved in making a product does not impact its cost?

posts.name: Ⓑⓞⓑⓑⓨ Beachy
posts.cooked:
Knut_Sogner:

Many things will affect the wine prices in the future, but labour costs will not be one of them.

If you are going to imply that “market” forces are independent of labor costs, I’m not sure I understand. Labor costs are also a product of demand, and not just in the vacuum of economic factors we’ve been discussing. Wine job wages are a result of things happening within the industry and also in completely unrelated or partially related industries. All of the variables and costs work against and in concert with one another to establish the capability of producing wine of a certain quality, and the demand, in the case of wine, is a reflection of the desire for the wines that the market is capable of producing. If demand decreases, it doesn’t necessarily mean that wine prices (and/or wages and costs) will lower significantly if wage earners can easily find work elsewhere and profitability is impossible. It might mean that people leave the business instead.

posts.name: Knut Sogner
posts.cooked: The labour impacts the cost. No doubt about it. Which is why previous respected wine producing regions are fallow. The costs are real. You can add it all up. But the labour cost does not - or to a much lesser degree - impact the price for wine that is valued far above the cost of making that wine, which is priced as a consequence of supply and demand. All of this is made opaque to the maker - who is an individual who is somewhat oblivious to what goes around in the wider market - who communicates with a number of representatives who are also somwhat in the dark. So a secondary markets evolves, auctions, other avenues, and «a market price» is set. Until this spring that has relentlessly gone upwards, for reasons of demand, which is why prices for producers have gone up (which again justifies costly production improvements). Wine, today, from certain regions, are priced way above production cost. And, I might add, a lot of clever people believe this luxury market is here to stay which is why they buy Bouchard Père et fils and properties like that. Maybe they are right and my «predictions» for a turn in the market will not account to anything. But my point is that demand - of an unhealthy speculative kind, I believe, but stand to be corrected - drives prices.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Knut_Sogner:

But my point is that demand - of an unhealthy speculative kind, I believe, but stand to be corrected - drives prices.

I see a lot of assumptions that wine prices are driven by “speculation” or something to that effect, but rarely any substantive evidence, so this tends to be difficult for me to accept. I would add that the initial price at which the wine is sold will be dependent on the labor costs and, of course, most wine sold in Burgundy isn’t especially expensive nor subject to much speculation. 1er crus and grand crus form less than 10% of all Burgundy, after all.

posts.name: Knut Sogner
posts.cooked: I agree with this, see my reply the Greg K. below. I admit to making my argument pointed, but I did that because I think in terms of the most extreme price increases we have seen is justified.

posts.name: Knut Sogner
posts.cooked: I am not sure what you are saying here.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Knut_Sogner:

I am not sure what you are saying here.

  1. You can’t ignore labor costs when considering prices. Supply and demand isn’t determined in a vacuum - producers won’t sell wines at a loss because the market clearing price is below cost; they’ll stop making wine.
  2. There’s not much evidence prices are based on “speculation”, especially when considering most Burgundy is not especially expensive.

posts.name: P eter L owet
posts.cooked:
My head hurts. While Knut is correct that pricing theory is generally independent of the cost of production, it’s really only true when the market-clearing price is above the marginal costs of production. Obviously if a producer would lose less money by walking away and not harvesting/producing/bottling wine, there won’t be a supply that we can complain about!

WK has laid out the price floor for a viable market for an artisanal, conscientious product. That’s fascinating and sobering, but it doesn’t really have much to do with why many burgundies sell for 10x-100x the cost. That’s when we get into the arena that lots of wealthy people are willing to pay a lot for wine. I hate it, but I’m not disgusted by it. Supply and demand is a b*tch. (It’s a different thread about whether making wine a rich man’s sport is good for the industry’s long-term sustainability.)

There is LESS info asymmetry than before – that’s why it’s a bummer for those of us who personally knew some wines that were priced far below the value we derived from drinking them. Hard to argue that critics who reveal that certain wines are damn good is the crux of the problem, but Donald will keeping trying!

posts.name: P eter L owet
posts.cooked: Oh crap, Greg beat me to it.

posts.cooked: I’m just shocked you can use the land for free.

  2. in all wine regions most wine produced are dirt cheap. We are of course talking about premier and grand cru here, with the additional lieu dits.

posts.name: John Maguire
posts.cooked: I think @Scott_Watkins either on this or another thread made an argument just short of “don’t feed the trolls.” I will go further: a bot can generate nonsense arguments far faster than reasonable people can knock them down. I am putting this thread on ignore and offer this advice as an attempt at a PSA.

reply_to_user.name: M a r k K a p l a n

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
This is an interesting summary but unfortunately he is not the arbiter of worldwide taste. As much as England especially thinks they are foody nation, well except for Dehlia Smith, they are not.

What we are seeing now is quite simple with Champagnes at least. Producers release champagnes at the beginning of the year. The Wine advocate points arrive mid year, producers feel on this basis they can increase prices. The traditional sellers and customers are alienated, those buying on the basis of inflated points are disappointed. They do not buy again.

Producers at first buoyant with the hype, come to wonder why the traditional markets are not buying anymore, the new point hungry buyers move on to the next hyped producer, the next producer with inflated 95 points and these champagnes appear on instagram and then those posters now move on the next.
Normally we have to chase producers, countless calls and emails, collect the champagnes in the evening after they come back from the fields, but now at some point they come back to us, why are you not taking our champagnes anymore? We will even deliver, they say?

In the meantime the guy in the Sezannes is turning into the Champagnes George the third, or as one colleague recently said, he is off his rocker. William, you have to help him, you partially got him into this mess and he lacks Egly’s grandeur and business savvy.

reply_to_user.name: M a r k K a p l a n

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Very well put!

There is still an awful lot of Burgundy below or around 50 EUR, and the purpose of my post was to explain to people what you can, and can’t, get for that price point or less.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Which is not true, as outside investors are buying lots of land in Burgundy, at prices that are out of synch with what the wine that can be produced on them in many cases.

posts.name: Knut Sogner
posts.cooked: I know I was harsh on your considered post, but it was possible to read it so you implied that «non-expensive» wines justified all prices. Still, EUR50 is not cheap, and that is whar you have to pay for Bourgogne rouge and blanc in my market. And great Italian producers easily undercuts that in value in my book.

posts.name: Knut Sogner
posts.cooked: The rise is land prices is part of the sustained push for higher wine prices. The LMVH effect of creating «timeless luxury»?

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Knut_Sogner:

The rise is land prices is part of the sustained push for higher wine prices. The LMVH effect of creating «timeless luxury»?

This is backwards though.

posts.name: Knut Sogner
posts.cooked: I do not understand? Lmvh is obviously a player on the demand side of Burgundy. Buying land and selling the product is, of course, affecting the price. I have not argued that supply does not matter. Price is set by supply meeting demand. I argued that labour costs did not matter for the huge price rises of premium Burgundy.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: As one top producer in Meursault observed to me a few months ago, ‘our “outil de production” has become a speculative asset for outsiders’. So yes, I agree with Greg, you have it inverted.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Not if you took the time to read it. I began by observing that most regions are forced into a race to the bottom, but that in a few, consumers are prepared to pay for excellence. I then enumerated the base costs of excellence, by one definition, in the Côte d’Or.

posts.name: Knut Sogner
posts.cooked: I have not implied that Burgundy producers themselves have created the extraordinary price rises. Nor have I criticized them for rising their own prices when the secondary market have continued to increase the price level. Lmvh is one of these players. I have talked about the mechanisms. Me, I am just a fan who has travelled to Burgundy regularly for thirty years (next year).

posts.name: Knut Sogner
posts.cooked: There has been a healthy and regular rise in prices for many regions that have suffered in the past. You need to be hard-working and knowledgable, but makers from Languedoc, Sicily, Croatia, not to speak of Beaujolais, are increasingly carving out a decent way of living from making wine.

posts.name: Knut Sogner
posts.cooked: I stand by my original reply to William Kelley as a response to a general argument for why wine prices in prestigeous regions, and especially Burgundy, have risen so much recently. This is a great concern to me. I do, however, want to point out - apologise is the best word - for my rough treatment of what he had stated. I might excuse myself by stating that I think his post was apologetic for some of what has happened, but this - as well as a general recognition of his subtle points - should have been clarified and come through with the required nuance.

posts.name: P eter L owet
posts.cooked: Let me clarify: It’s a function of projected future prices, not current ones. Investors/speculators will presumably pay based on their expectations of future value of the land (which is based on production levels, costs and prices). You and I might be shocked by their bullish outlook, but that’s beside the point.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
In November Chatgpt gave us a glimpse of the AI they are offering corporate firms, what we have is nothing compared to what they have. AI is in google, in your phone, it is literally everywhere.

Next to my shop, there is a job agency. The owner told me in Germany, firms are releuctant to hire people at the moment as they just do not know where the job market is going to go with the emergence of AI. The reckoning at the moment, the whole job landscape is going to be completly different in five years time.

This is here and it is not going to go away, better to embrace it now before it is too late.
